To solve the problem, we need to determine the minimum number of tables required for Marco to invite friends such that no two friends who together have all \( n \) stickers are seated at the same table.

### Part (i): Show that Marco might need to reserve at least \( n \) different tables.

Consider a scenario where each friend has exactly one unique sticker. For example, Friend 1 has sticker 1, Friend 2 hasicker 2, and so on up to Friend \( n \) who hasicker \( n \). In this case, any pair of friends will together have exactly two stickers. However, if we consider the pair of friends who have stickers \( i \) and \( j \), their combined collection will not include all \( n \) stickers unless \( i \) and \( j \) are such that their combined stickers cover all \( n \) stickers. 

To ensure that no two friends who together have all \( n \) stickers are seated at the same table, we need to assign each friend to a different table. This is because if any two friends are seated at the same table, their combined collection might include all \( n \) stickers, which would violate Marco's requirement. Therefore, in the worst case, Marco might need to reserve at least \( n \) different tables.

### Part (ii): Show that \( n \) tables will always be sufficient for Marco to achieve his desire.

To show that \( n \) tables are sufficient, we can use a coloring argument. Assign each friend to a table based on the sticker they have. Specifically, if a friend hasicker \( i \), assign them to table \( i \). 

Now, consider any two friends seated at the same table. Since each table corresponds to a unique sticker, any two friends at the same table will have the same sticker. Therefore, their combined collection will not include all \( n \) stickers because they are missing at least \( n-1 \) stickers. This ensures that no two friends who together have all \( n \) stickers are seated at the same table.

Thus, \( n \) tables are always sufficient for Marco to achieve his desire.

### Final Answer

(i) \boxed{n}
